subject: math
source_title: New General Mathematics SSS 1 by M.F. Macrae et al
learning_objectives: Draw graphs of quadratic functions using a table of values
Read the roots of a quadratic equation from its graph
Identify minimum and maximum values from a quadratic graph
Determine the line of symmetry of a parabola
Find the equation of a parabola from its graph

sections.heading: Steps for Drawing a Quadratic Graph
sections.content: To draw a quadratic graph: (1) use the given range of x-values (or choose a suitable one); (2) calculate corresponding y-values and prepare a table; (3) choose a suitable scale; (4) draw axes and plot points; (5) join points with a smooth curve (parabola).

sections.heading: Reading Roots, Maximum and Minimum Values
sections.content: The roots of the equation are the x-values where the curve crosses the x-axis (where y = 0). The curve is n-shaped (maximum) when the coefficient of x² is negative, and V-shaped (minimum) when positive. The maximum value of y is the highest point; the minimum is the lowest point. A curve may: cross the x-axis at two points (two real roots), touch it once (equal/repeated roots), or not cross it at all (no real roots, imaginary roots).

sections.heading: Line of Symmetry
sections.content: The line of symmetry divides the parabola into two equal halves. It passes through the vertex (maximum or minimum point) and is a vertical line x = constant. For example, for y = 4x² − 12x + 9, the curve touches the x-axis at x = 1.5 (twice), and the line of symmetry is x = 1.5.

sections.heading: Finding an Equation from a Graph
sections.content: If a curve crosses the x-axis at points a and b, the equation is obtained from (x − a)(x − b) = 0. The y-intercept (when x = 0) is used to scale the equation if needed. Example: if roots are x = −2 and x = 1/2, the expression (x + 2)(x − 1/2) = 0 gives x² + (3/2)x − 1 = 0. Multiplying by 2 to match the y-intercept: 2x² + 3x − 2 = 0.
